confirmed.title: Tajweed color map keys don't match Quran.com class names — most tajweed rules render with no color
confirmed.description: The TAJWEED_COLORS map uses class names (qalqala, ghunna, ikhfa, ikhfa_shafawi, iqlab, idgham_ghunna, idgham_no_ghunna, idgham_mutaqaribain) that do not match what the Quran.com uthmani_tajweed edition actually emits. Live API verification (chapters 1, 112, 113) shows the API emits `qalaqah`, `ghunnah`, `ikhafa`, `idgham_wo_ghunnah`, `iqlb`, `idgham_ghunnah`, etc. parse() looks up `TAJWEED_COLORS[c.trim()]` and finds nothing, so qalqalah, ghunnah, ikhfa, iqlab and all idgham rules — the core tajweed rules — render as plain uncolored text. Only ham_wasl, laam_shamsiyah, slnt and the madda_* rules ever get colored. The 'Tajweed colors' toggle is therefore mostly a no-op and fails silently.
confirmed.evidence: Map: `qalqala: '#DD0008',` / `ghunna: '#FF8000',` / `ikhfa: '#9B4900',` / `idgham_no_ghunna: '#9BC124',` — but live API response for 113 returns classes: `1 class=ghunnah / 4 class=ikhafa / 5 class=qalaqah` and 112 returns `<tajweed class=qalaqah>د</tajweed>` and `<tajweed class=idgham_wo_ghunnah>`; lookup `TAJWEED_COLORS[c.trim()]` misses all of them.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add the API's actual class names as keys (qalaqah, ghunnah, ikhafa, ikhafa_shafawi, iqlb, idgham_ghunnah, idgham_wo_ghunnah, idgham_mutaqaribayn), keeping the old keys as aliases. Verify against a dump of all distinct classes across the 114 chapters.
confirmed.correction: The core bug is real, but the claim's API evidence is partly wrong: the API emits `iqlab` (not `iqlb`), which DOES match the map's `iqlab` key, so iqlab is colored correctly; `idgham_shafawi` also matches and is colored. So the accurate statement is: six emitted classes miss the map (ghunnah, ikhafa, ikhafa_shafawi, qalaqah, idgham_ghunnah, idgham_wo_ghunnah), while ham_wasl, slnt, laam_shamsiyah, iqlab, idgham_shafawi, and the four emitted madda_* classes all color correctly — not "only ham_wasl, laam_shamsiyah, slnt and madda_*" as claimed. The fix is renaming/aliasing those six keys (keeping the old spellings as aliases is harmless).

confirmed.title: Duplicate Bismillah at the top of every Uthmani-script surah (default mode for non-Indo-Pak users)
confirmed.description: alquran.cloud's quran-uthmani edition prepends the Bismillah to ayah 1 of every surah except 1 and 9 (verified live: surah 2 ayah 1 text is 'بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ الٓمٓ'). fetchFromNetwork stores `a.text` unmodified, and QuranScreen renders its own fixed Bismillah header above the ayah list for every surah != 9. Result: in plain Uthmani mode (the default script for every user outside Indo-Pak timezones), the Bismillah appears twice — once as the styled header and again fused into ayah 1, displayed under the ayah-1 number badge. IndoPak and tajweed texts come from Quran.com which does not include it, so those modes are unaffected — making this a script-dependent inconsistency in the core Quran text display.
confirmed.evidence: QuranScreen.tsx: `{selectedSurah.number !== 9 && (<Text style={[styles.bismillah, ...]}>{bismillahText}</Text>)}` then renders `{indopakText ?? ayah.arabic}`; services/quran.ts maps `arabic: a.text` with no stripping; live API: data[0].ayahs[0].text for surah 2 = 'بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ الٓمٓ'.
confirmed.suggestedFix: In fetchFromNetwork, strip the leading Bismillah from ayah 1 for surahs 2-114 (excluding 9 which has none), e.g. remove the known prefix string (and the U+FEFF BOM present on 1:1) before caching. Bump CACHE_VERSION so poisoned caches refresh.
confirmed.correction: Claim is accurate with two minor refinements: (1) IndoPak and tajweed modes are only unaffected when the Quran.com texts have loaded — the `?? ayah.arabic` fallback (offline / before fetch resolves) reintroduces the duplicate in those modes too; (2) the page-based 604-page Mushaf reader uses Quran.com text_uthmani and is unaffected, so the bug is confined to the surah reader (both its verse and continuous modes), as the claim implies.

confirmed.title: openSurah has no staleness guard — rapid surah switching shows the wrong surah's text under the new header
confirmed.description: openSurah awaits fetchSurah and unconditionally calls setSurahContent/setError when it resolves, with no check that the user is still on that surah. With no fetch timeout, a slow request for surah A can resolve long after the user backed out and opened surah B (which hit cache instantly): A's content then overwrites B's — header/title show B while Arabic text and translations are A's, and the audio player (driven by selectedSurah.number = B with surahContent = A's ayahs) recites surah B verses against surah A text. Similarly, A's late rejection sets the error banner on top of B's successfully loaded content. This is an intermittent wrong-content bug in the #1 complaint area.
confirmed.evidence: const openSurah = useCallback(async (surah: SurahMeta) => { ... try { const content = await fetchSurah(surah.number); setSurahContent(content); prefetchAdjacent(surah.number); } catch { setError(...); } finally { setLoading(false); } }, [isUrdu, stop]); — no `if (selectedSurahRef.current !== surah.number) return;` style guard before any setState.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Keep a ref/request-id of the latest opened surah; in the then/catch, bail out if the resolved surah no longer matches (same cancelled-flag pattern already used in the tajweed/indopak effects).

confirmed.title: No timeout on any Quran fetch, and the inflight-dedupe map makes the Retry button a no-op while a request hangs
confirmed.description: fetchFromNetwork, fetchPage, fetchIndopakTexts and fetchTajweedTexts all call bare fetch() with no AbortController/timeout and no retry. On a flaky mobile network (the app's core Pakistan/India audience) a request can hang for 60+ seconds, leaving the user on 'Loading surah...' indefinitely. Worse: fetchSurah dedupes via the `inflight` map and only deletes the entry in `finally` — so while the original request is hung, tapping the error-screen Retry button (or reopening the surah) just returns the same stuck promise. The user has no way to force a fresh request. This directly explains 'sometimes translation doesn't load'.
confirmed.evidence: const res = await fetch(url); (line 58, no timeout/AbortController) and: `const existing = inflight.get(surahNumber); if (existing) return existing;` — Retry calls openSurah → fetchSurah → returns the same hung promise; inflight.delete only runs in `finally` after the hang resolves.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Wrap fetches with AbortController + ~15s timeout and one retry with backoff; on timeout the inflight entry clears so Retry issues a genuinely new request.
confirmed.correction: Accurate version: all Quran fetches lack timeouts/AbortController, so on RN Android (infinite OkHttp timeouts) a hung request leaves the user on an indefinite 'Loading surah...' spinner with no Retry button visible; backing out and reopening the surah joins the same hung inflight promise, so there is no way to force a fresh request until the original settles. But the Retry button itself is never a no-op — it only appears after the promise rejects, by which point the inflight entry has been deleted in `finally`, so Retry always issues a fresh fetch. Severity is closer to medium: real reliability gap (infinite spinner, no recovery mid-hang) but the broken-Retry mechanism described does not exist, and the link to 'sometimes translation doesn't load' is speculative.

confirmed.title: Audio: no generation guard around Sound.createAsync — overlapping double playback and phantom audio after Stop
confirmed.description: playSeg awaits Audio.Sound.createAsync({ shouldPlay: true }) and only afterwards assigns soundRef.current. Two failure modes: (1) Tap ayah 5 then quickly tap ayah 9 while 5 is still loading — both createAsync calls run; the loser's sound is never stored in soundRef and never unloaded, so two recitations play simultaneously, and BOTH chains keep auto-advancing via onStatus.didJustFinish. (2) Press Stop (or back) while loading — stop() nulls soundRef and resets state, then the inflight createAsync resolves and starts playing with currentIndex already null, so the player bar is hidden and the user hears audio they cannot stop. Matches the 'random things fail' complaint class.
confirmed.evidence: setPbStatus('loading'); const oldSound = soundRef.current; soundRef.current = null; if (oldSound) oldSound.unloadAsync().catch(() => {}); ... const { sound } = await Audio.Sound.createAsync({ uri: url }, { shouldPlay: true }, onStatus); soundRef.current = sound; — nothing between the await and the assignment checks whether stop() ran or a newer playSeg superseded this one (segRef.current is overwritten before the await).
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add a monotonically increasing playToken ref; capture it before createAsync and after the await, if the token changed (stop/new play), unload the freshly created sound and return.
confirmed.correction: One detail is overstated: "BOTH chains keep auto-advancing" is not sustained. When either chain's clip finishes, its didJustFinish handler calls playSeg(next), whose slow path unloads whatever sound currently sits in soundRef — i.e., the other chain's sound — so one chain kills the other at the first advance. Actual behavior: overlapping double playback for the duration of the first clip, then an unpredictable single survivor (possibly the chain for the ayah the user tapped first, not last), with the other cut mid-clip. Also, in the Stop scenario the player bar reappears once the phantom clip finishes and the chain auto-advances (playSeg re-sets currentIndex), so "audio they cannot stop" lasts one clip, after which the resumed (wrong) playback is stoppable. Neither nuance changes the verdict; the missing-guard bug and high severity stand.

confirmed.title: JUZ_START_PAGE is off by one page for 22 of 30 juz — 'para N' search and legacy juz resume land on the previous juz
confirmed.description: Verified against the same Quran.com API the Mushaf reader renders from: juz 8, 9, 10 and 12-30 all start one page later than this table claims (e.g. juz 30 starts on page 582, table says 581; juz 12 → 222 vs 221; juz 23 → 442 vs 441). Searching 'para 30' or 'پارہ 30' therefore opens the last page of juz 29, and the legacy juz→page last-read migration in loadLastRead resumes users one page before their para. Juz 1-7 and 11 happen to be correct.

confirmed.title: Tajweed text renders the API's <span class=end> verse digit, duplicating verse numbers
confirmed.description: Every uthmani_tajweed verse ends with `١` (verified live). The parser's second regex alternative `<[^>]*>` eats the span tags but the Arabic-Indic digit between them matches the plain-text alternative and is rendered. QuranScreen then appends its own ` ﴿N﴾ ` marker after each TajweedText, so with Tajweed on, every ayah shows its number twice (e.g. '...أَحَدٌ ١ ﴿1﴾').
confirmed.evidence: API: `"text_uthmani_tajweed":"قُلْ هُوَ ... أَحَدٌ ١"`; regex `/([^<]*)<\/tajweed>|<[^>]*>|([^<]+)/g` — `١` falls into group 5 and is pushed: `} else if (m[5] !== undefined) { result.push({ text: m[5] }); }`; QuranScreen line 768 then adds `{` ﴿${ayah.numberInSurah}﴾ `}`.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Track when the previous match was a `` opening tag and skip the following text run, or strip `[^<]*` from the raw string before parsing.
confirmed.correction: Claim is accurate for mushaf view (line 768 inline ﴿N﴾ marker → true inline duplication). In the card/list view (lines 822-825) the verse number is in a separate badge rather than inline, so there the symptom is a stray trailing digit in the Arabic text plus the badge — still a visible defect, just not strictly an inline double marker.

confirmed.title: AyahSheet overwrites the global audio mode, disabling background playback for the main surah player
confirmed.description: expo-av's audio mode is global. useAudioPlayer sets staysActiveInBackground: true once on mount so surah recitation continues with the screen locked. AyahSheet.playAyah then calls setAudioModeAsync with staysActiveInBackground: false — so after the user plays a single ayah from the Mushaf sheet, the surah reader's continuous recitation silently stops at screen-lock for the rest of the session (the hook never re-sets the mode). An intermittent, hard-to-reproduce regression of exactly the kind users report.
confirmed.evidence: AyahSheet.tsx: `await Audio.setAudioModeAsync({ playsInSilentModeIOS: true, staysActiveInBackground: false, shouldDuckAndroid: true });` vs useAudioPlayer.ts lines 228-234 (mount-only): `Audio.setAudioModeAsync({ playsInSilentModeIOS: true, staysActiveInBackground: true, shouldDuckAndroid: true })`.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Use the same audio mode in both places (staysActiveInBackground: true is harmless for the one-shot ayah clip), or re-apply the player's mode when playback starts in useAudioPlayer rather than only on mount.
confirmed.correction: Minor nuance only: the mode is restored not just by app restart but also whenever QuranTab remounts QuranScreen via the pendingSurah deep-link mountKey bump (App.tsx:153-162), so "for the rest of the session" slightly overstates persistence. Practically, in normal in-tab use the broken mode does persist session-long. Otherwise the claim is accurate as stated.

confirmed.title: Mushaf page error state has no retry — error text persists after reconnecting
confirmed.description: When a page fails to load offline, MushafPage shows 'Reconnect and try again' but offers no retry button, and the fetch effect only re-runs on pageNum/isUrdu change. With windowSize=3 the adjacent pages stay mounted, so after reconnecting, swiping to the failed page still shows the stale error; the only recovery is swiping 2+ pages away so virtualization unmounts it, or leaving the reader. The copy promises a retry path that does not exist.
confirmed.evidence: if (error) { return (<View style={[styles.page, styles.loadingWrap]}><Text style={styles.errorText}>{error}</Text></View>); } — no TouchableOpacity/retry, and the only trigger is `useEffect(... , [pageNum, isUrdu])`.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add a Retry button that re-runs the fetch (e.g. a `retryNonce` state in the effect deps), matching the surah reader's error UI.

confirmed.title: Stale 'Continue Reading' card: lastRead loaded once in bare useEffect on a keep-alive screen and not refreshed on most back paths
confirmed.description: lastRead is read from storage only in a mount-time useEffect. The screen stays mounted across tab switches (keep-alive), and: (1) opening a surah calls saveLastRead but never setLastRead, and both back paths from the surah reader (header back at line 625, hardware back at 577-586) just clear selectedSurah without reloading — so after reading Surah Yasin the landing card still advertises the previous session's position; (2) on Android hardware back from the Mushaf reader, QuranScreen's own BackHandler (registered after the child's, so invoked first) runs `setSelectedPage(null)` directly, bypassing the onBack prop that contains the loadLastRead refresh — so the card also goes stale for page reading on Android back.
confirmed.evidence: Mount-only load: `useEffect(() => { (async () => { ... loadLastRead(), ... if (lr) setLastRead(lr); ... })(); }, []);` — back button: `onPress={async () => { await stop(); setSelectedSurah(null); setSurahContent(null); }}` and hardware back: `if (selectedPage !== null) { setSelectedPage(null); } else { setSelectedSurah(null); setSurahContent(null); }` — neither calls loadLastRead/setLastRead; only the MushafPageReader onBack prop (lines 596-601) refreshes.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Call setLastRead({type:'surah', value, ts}) inside openSurah, and refresh lastRead in every path that returns to the landing (or derive the card from a small in-memory store updated by saveLastRead).
confirmed.correction: Two minor nuances: (a) the keep-alive framing understates the surah-path bug — it occurs within a single screen session and does not depend on tab switching at all; (b) the Android back-handler ordering claim holds for the common path, but because onBack is an inline arrow prop, any QuranScreen re-render while the Mushaf reader is open (e.g., changing script) re-subscribes the child's handler last, in which case the child's onBack (with the refresh) runs instead. The bug is therefore intermittent on that path, though present in the default open-then-back flow.

confirmed.title: Verse search '2:255' claims to open the ayah but opens the surah at the top
confirmed.description: The smart-search hint for a verse reference renders 'Open Al-Baqarah 2:255', but tapping it only calls openSurah(s) — the parsed ayah number is discarded and there is no scroll-to-ayah. For Al-Baqarah the user lands at ayah 1 and must scroll through 254 ayahs manually. The ayah number is also never validated against the surah's ayah count ('2:999' produces the same confident hint).
confirmed.evidence: } else if (intent.type === 'verse') { const s = SURAH_LIST.find((x) => x.number === intent.surah); if (s) { openSurah(s); setSearch(''); } } — `intent.ayah` is never used for navigation; parse only checks `a >= 1`.
confirmed.suggestedFix: After surahContent loads, scroll to ayahYRef.current[ayah-1] (the layout map already exists for the audio autoscroll); validate intent.ayah <= s.ayahs before showing the hint.

confirmed.title: IndoPak/tajweed fetch failures fall back silently — toggle stays on while showing the other script, no feedback or retry
confirmed.description: fetchIndopakTexts and fetchTajweedTexts return [] on any HTTP failure or exception. In QuranScreen the render falls back via `indopakText ?? ayah.arabic` / `tajweedTexts[idx] ?? ayah.arabic`, so a user who selected Indo-Pak script (the default for Urdu/Indo-Pak users) on a flaky connection sees Uthmani text while the 'Indo-Pak' pill shows selected — with no error, no retry, and no refetch until they switch surahs. Same for the Tajweed toggle, which stays ON while rendering zero colors. Silent script/feature mismatch in the exact 'random things fail' category.
confirmed.evidence: if (!res.ok) return []; ... catch { // Offline + no cache: return empty so the reader falls back to the // Uthmani text ... return []; } — and QuranScreen line 766: `<Text ...>{indopakText ?? ayah.arabic}</Text>` with the pill still rendering `script === 'indopak'` as active.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Surface a small inline banner ('Indo-Pak text unavailable offline — showing Madinah script. Retry') when the texts array comes back empty while that script/toggle is active, and retry automatically on next focus/connectivity.
confirmed.correction: Two minor nuances: (1) "no refetch until they switch surahs" is slightly overstated — toggling the script pill away and back, or toggling Tajweed off/on, re-runs the effects and retries the fetch (deps include script/tajweedOn); nothing in the UI hints at this though. (2) Successful fetches are cached per-surah in AsyncStorage indefinitely, so the failure window is only the first read of a given surah on a bad connection — consistent with the stated medium severity.

confirmed.title: Surah cache stores missing translations as '' and is validated only by ayah count — one bad response blanks translations for that surah permanently
confirmed.description: fetchFromNetwork coalesces missing per-ayah translation entries to empty strings (english.ayahs[idx]?.text ?? '') and writeCache persists the result forever (fetchedAt is never checked; there is no TTL). fetchSurah treats any cache with ayahs.length > 0 as valid, never checking that translations are present. If alquran.cloud ever returns a truncated/partial translation edition with a 200 (the arabic edition intact), the surah is cached with blank urdu/english and every subsequent open serves the poisoned cache — translation 'never loads' for that surah until a manual cache-version bump or app-data clear. The reader renders the empty string with no 'translation unavailable' fallback (unlike AyahSheet).
confirmed.evidence: english: english.ayahs[idx]?.text ?? '', urdu: urdu.ayahs[idx]?.text ?? '', → writeCache(content); and the read path: `if (cached && cached.ayahs && cached.ayahs.length > 0) return cached;` — no check on translation content; QuranScreen line 828 renders `{ayah.urdu}` with no empty-string fallback.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Validate before caching (every ayah has non-empty arabic and at least one translation; lengths of all three editions match), and/or treat cache entries with empty translations as a miss so they refetch.
confirmed.correction: Claim is accurate as written. Minor nuances: (1) a fully missing translation edition is guarded (`editions.length < 3` throws), so only a present-but-truncated/empty edition triggers poisoning — consistent with the claim's scenario; (2) the same persist-forever, length-only-validation pattern also exists in fetchPage for the Mushaf reader, though AyahSheet's fallback masks blank translations there at display time.

confirmed.title: AyahSheet 'Pause' button actually stops and discards the sound; load/playback errors are swallowed silently
confirmed.description: While an ayah is playing the button is labeled '⏸ Pause' but onPress calls stopAyah, which stops AND unloads the sound — tapping play again redownloads and restarts from the beginning. Separately, if Sound.createAsync fails (offline, everyayah down), the catch block just resets state: the spinner disappears and nothing happens, with no error message — another silent 'random failure'.
confirmed.evidence: onPress={playing ? stopAyah : playAyah} with label `{playing ? ... '⏸  Pause' : ...}` while stopAyah does `await soundRef.current?.stopAsync(); await soundRef.current?.unloadAsync(); soundRef.current = null;`; playAyah error path: `} catch { setPlaying(false); }` — no user-visible feedback.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Use pauseAsync/playAsync for the toggle (mirroring useAudioPlayer.togglePlayPause), and show a brief inline error ('Couldn't load audio — check connection') in the catch.
confirmed.correction: Minor wording nuance only: "redownloads" is approximately right — the app re-streams the remote URI from position 0 with no app-level caching; whether bytes are actually re-fetched depends on platform HTTP cache, but playback always restarts from the beginning.

confirmed.title: Opening the app early on an event day cancels the already-scheduled day-of sacred notification
confirmed.description: scheduleSacredCountdownNotifications first cancels ALL 'sc:' notifications, then skips any event whose effectiveDate (midnight local) is <= now. On the event day itself (e.g. Eid), the day-0 milestone fires at 6:30am — but if the user opens the app between 00:00 and 06:30 (typical Fajr-time usage, ~4-5am), the reschedule runs (PrayerTrackerScreen's effect re-runs on the day-change key), the previously scheduled 6:30am 'Eid is today' notification is cancelled by the cleanup loop, and the skip condition prevents it from being re-created because midnight has already passed. The per-milestone guard `if (trigger <= now) continue` at line 547 already handles past triggers correctly, so the event-level skip is both redundant and destructive.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Compare against end of the event day instead of midnight: skip only when `endOfDay(event.effectiveEndDate ?? event.effectiveDate) < now` (or drop the event-level skip entirely and rely on the existing `if (trigger <= now) continue` per-milestone guard).

confirmed.title: Prayer tracker blocks behind an unbounded GPS fix — full-screen spinner even when cached location exists
confirmed.description: useLocation sets the cached location into state early, but keeps `loading` true until `Location.getCurrentPositionAsync()` resolves — and that call has no timeout, no `maximumAge`, and no fallback timer (expo-location's getCurrentPositionAsync is known to stall indefinitely indoors / with location services degraded on Android). PrayerTrackerScreen renders ONLY a full-screen spinner while `locationLoading` is true, so users with a perfectly good cached location stare at 'loading prayer times' for the full duration of a GPS fix, or forever if it hangs. This matches the user complaint 'sometimes random things fail'.
confirmed.suggestedFix: In useLocation, call `setLoading(false)` as soon as cached coords are applied (treat the GPS fix as a background refresh), and wrap getCurrentPositionAsync in a Promise.race timeout (e.g. 10s) falling back to `Location.getLastKnownPositionAsync()`.
confirmed.correction: Claim is accurate with one refinement: the indefinite-hang case only occurs when getCurrentPositionAsync stalls without resolving or rejecting; a rejection (e.g., location services off) IS handled by the catch block with cached/Karachi fallback. The certain, reproducible issue is that the full-screen spinner blocks for the full duration of every fresh GPS fix even when cached coordinates were already loaded into state. Severity high is defensible for the stall case, medium-high for the typical case.

confirmed.title: Marking a prayer within ~60s of resuming after midnight writes it to yesterday's record
confirmed.description: `today` is only advanced by a 60-second setInterval. JS timers are suspended while the app is backgrounded, so when the user resumes the app on a new day (e.g. opens it for Fajr after it was backgrounded overnight), `today` — and therefore `todayKey` used by markPrayer — remains yesterday's date for up to 60 seconds. A Fajr tap in that window is recorded under yesterday's key: today's row stays unmarked, yesterday gets a phantom prayer, and the streak/week dots are wrong. There is no AppState 'active' or focus-driven refresh of `today` (the keep-alive bug class applied to the date instead of storage).
confirmed.suggestedFix: Also refresh `today` in a useFocusEffect and in an AppState 'active' listener (and/or compute the date key inside markPrayer with `getLocalDateKey(new Date())` at tap time).

confirmed.title: Saving prayer settings silently flips Shia users back to 'sunni' fiqh when using any non-Jafari method
confirmed.description: onSettingsSave derives fiqhSchool purely from the calculation method: anything other than 'Jafari' overwrites storage.setFiqhSchool('sunni'). A Shia user who picks the Tehran method (Iran's official method, a perfectly normal Shia choice) or any regional method and taps Save gets their fiqh flipped to sunni — which then changes Sacred Countdown content (hides Arbaeen, switches Mawlid date to the Sunni one) and overrides the sect they explicitly chose in onboarding, the Janaza screen, or the fiqh pills in this very modal. PrayerSettingsModal even shows separate fiqh pills that write setFiqhSchool independently, so Save actively undoes the user's pill selection.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Read the current fiqhSchool from storage (or pass the modal's fiqhSchool state through onSave) and only auto-set it when the user explicitly toggled the school pills; never infer 'sunni' from the calc method alone.

confirmed.title: Scheduled notification count (~67) exceeds iOS 64-pending limit and the code's own stated ~50 Android budget
confirmed.description: The code budgets for ~50 pending notifications, but the actual total is larger: 35 prayer reminders (7 days x 5) + sacred countdown milestones + streak daily + Jumu'ah weekly. Counting from today's date (2026-06-10, Sunni/southasia): the 12 sliced events (hijri, ashura, mawlid, isra, shab-e-barat, ramadan [6 milestones], 5x qadr, eid-fitr [4]) yield ~30 sacred notifications, for a total of ~67. On iOS the system keeps only the soonest-firing 64 and silently discards the rest, so the furthest-out sacred milestones (the 30/14-day Ramadan anticipation ladder, Eid reminders) are dropped without error. The comment's own arithmetic ('7 x 5 = 35 entries leaves room under Android's ~50 pending-notification cap for streak + sacred schedules') is violated by the sacred scheduler it references.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Reduce the combined budget: e.g. cap sacred milestones to the next ~20 by trigger date, or drop PRAYER_SCHEDULE_DAYS_AHEAD to 5 (25 entries), keeping the total comfortably under 64.
confirmed.correction: Three refinements: (1) The total varies 62-67 by time of day (already-passed prayers on day 0 are skipped), so it only exceeds 64 when scheduling happens before roughly the day's third prayer — most but not all of the day. (2) The claim misidentifies the dropped notifications: iOS keeps the 64 soonest-firing, so the ~3 dropped are the FURTHEST-firing entries (Eid al-Fitr day-of/night-before and the last Laylat al-Qadr day-of in March 2027), not the 30/14-day Ramadan anticipation milestones, which fire in January 2027 and sit comfortably within the soonest 64. (3) Severity context: the app currently ships Android-only (eas Android production builds; iOS config exists in app.json but no iOS release), and Android's scheduled-alarm limit is ~500, not ~50 — the comment's "~50 pending-notification cap" conflates Android's posted-notification limit with scheduled ones. So today's practical impact is a violated comment contract plus latent iOS silent-drop risk that materializes if/when the iOS build ships; medium severity is at the high end for the current Android-only release, but the budget overflow itself is real and persistent (still ~71 max in Jan 2027).

confirmed.title: Concurrent cancel-then-schedule runs can interleave and leave a mixed/duplicated prayer-notification set
confirmed.description: schedulePrayerNotificationsAhead is a multi-step async sequence (read all pending, cancel pr:* one by one, then schedule ~35 new) with no serialization. It is invoked concurrently from independent call sites: App.tsx boot (line 444 with stored/Karachi coords, then line 460 again after GPS), the AppState resume listener (line 482), and PrayerTrackerScreen's effect (line 177) — which on first mount fires with the Karachi fallback coords (lat = location?.latitude ?? 24.8607) before the real location resolves. When two runs with different coordinates interleave, run B's cancel pass can miss notifications run A schedules after B's read, leaving both Karachi-time and real-location-time reminders pending simultaneously (different trigger times means different `pr:Name:timestamp` identifiers, so identifier-replacement does not dedupe them). Users get double adhan reminders at wrong times until the next clean rebuild.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Serialize through a module-level promise chain (let queue = Promise.resolve(); queue = queue.then(run)) inside schedulePrayerNotificationsAhead, and skip scheduling from PrayerTrackerScreen while location is still loading.
confirmed.correction: The bug is real but two cited race pairs are wrong: App.tsx lines 444 and 460 are sequential awaits inside the same async IIFE and cannot race each other; and PrayerTrackerScreen is not mounted at boot (it is the second, lazily-mounted tab — Home is initial), so its fallback-coords run fires on first open of the Prayers tab, not at launch. The realistic race is the screen effect's own Karachi-fallback run overlapping its location-resolved re-run (the effect's cancelled flag cannot abort an in-flight schedulePrayerNotificationsAhead), plus possible overlap with the boot IIFE or the AppState resume listener. Impact is also self-healing sooner than implied: the resume listener (App.tsx:482, 30s throttle) rebuilds the full pr:* set on every app foreground, so duplicates last only until the next resume. Severity medium is at the upper bound; medium-low is arguably more accurate given the narrow window (mostly fresh installs before GPS/cached location resolves).

confirmed.title: Location never refreshes during a session — keep-alive screen means travelers keep the old city's times and notifications
confirmed.description: useLocation fetches GPS in a bare useEffect keyed only on refreshTick, and PrayerTrackerScreen is a keep-alive tab screen that mounts once per app session. There is no useFocusEffect or AppState-resume re-fetch, and the manual `retry` is only reachable from the fallback banner (which is hidden when a cached location exists, since `setUsingFallback(false)` on cache hit). A user who flies Karachi → London with the app alive in memory keeps seeing Karachi prayer times, and the AppState resume rebuild (App.tsx:482) reschedules notifications from the same stale storage.getLocation(). This is the documented keep-alive bug class applied to location.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Trigger `retry()` from an AppState 'active' listener (throttled, e.g. once per 15 min) or from useFocusEffect on PrayerTrackerScreen, and reschedule notifications when coords move materially (> ~25 km).
confirmed.correction: Mostly accurate, with one missed escape hatch: QiblaScreen is a Stack.Screen (App.tsx:289-293) that also calls useLocation(), so every time the user opens the Qibla screen a fresh hook instance fetches GPS and writes storage.setLocation (useLocation.ts:75). That incidentally refreshes stored coords, so subsequent notification rebuilds would use the new city — but it never updates PrayerTrackerScreen's displayed times, whose hook instance keeps the stale location for the whole session. Also, the impact is limited to sessions where the OS keeps the process alive across the trip; a cold restart self-heals via App.tsx:448-462. Medium severity is fair.

confirmed.title: False 'Adhan reminders are off' banner persists after the user enables notifications in Settings
confirmed.description: When permission is denied, the effect sets notifDenied=true and renders a banner that deep-links to OS Settings. But after the user grants permission there and returns, nothing re-runs the effect: its deps are [today, language, calculationMethod, madhab, lat, lng], and none change on resume. The banner keeps claiming reminders are off (and sacred-countdown scheduling stays skipped) until the next day rollover, a settings change, or an app restart — undermining the very flow the banner asks the user to perform. (Prayer reminders themselves do recover via App.tsx's resume rebuild, making the stale banner doubly misleading.)
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add an AppState 'active' listener (or useFocusEffect) that re-checks Notifications.getPermissionsAsync() and clears notifDenied + retriggers scheduling when permission is now granted.

confirmed.title: markPrayer's storage write failure is unhandled — UI shows 'prayed' but the mark is lost on restart
confirmed.description: markPrayer updates React state first, then awaits storage.setPrayers with no try/catch and no caller handling (onPress={() => markPrayer(p.key)} discards the promise). If AsyncStorage write fails, the user sees the green 'Prayed' badge but the record silently never persists — the mark vanishes on next launch and breaks the streak. This failure mode is not hypothetical for this app: App.tsx documents that the Quran prefetch previously filled AsyncStorage to the 6MB SQLite cap 'which then blocked streak/goal/prayer writes with SQLITE_FULL'.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Wrap the write in try/catch; on failure revert the optimistic state (or retry once) and show a toast so the user knows the mark did not save.
confirmed.correction: The code-level finding is accurate, but the cited precedent overstates current likelihood: the SQLITE_FULL trigger has been remediated in this build — prefetchAllSurahs is now a no-op and App.tsx runs purgeQuranCacheOnce() to clear the bulk Quran cache (App.tsx:413-423). So the historical cause of write failures is gone; what remains is the generic unhandled-write-failure pattern (disk-full or other AsyncStorage errors), making this a valid but lower-probability medium/low reliability issue rather than an active recurrence of the documented incident.

confirmed.title: Prayer status badges go stale while the screen stays focused — a passed prayer stays 'upcoming'
confirmed.description: getStatus compares prayerTime against `new Date()` at render time, but nothing re-renders the screen as time passes: the 60s interval only calls setToday on a day change, and prayers state only changes on focus/tap/refresh. If the user leaves the Prayers tab open across a prayer time (keep-alive screens make this common), Maghrib still shows 'upcoming' minutes or hours after it passed, until they tab away and back or pull to refresh. NextEventCard solves the same problem with a per-minute tick; this screen doesn't.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Track a minute-tick state (like NextEventCard's setInterval(() => setNow(new Date()), 60_000)) or have the existing interval bump a tick state every minute, not just on day change.

confirmed.title: Unmarked prayer is labeled 'missed' the instant its adhan time passes, while its window is still open
confirmed.description: getStatus returns 'missed' (red badge, ○ icon) for any unmarked prayer whose start time has passed — even one minute after the adhan, when the prayer window legitimately extends until the next prayer. Islamically and practically, a user opening the app 20 minutes after Dhuhr to pray sees Dhuhr branded 'missed' in error red, which is both discouraging and incorrect. The 'missed' state should begin when the window closes (next prayer's time), not when it opens.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Treat a prayer as 'current/due' between its own time and the next prayer's time (sunrise for Fajr), and only show 'missed' after the window closes.

confirmed.title: Race: 'Read Surah' deep-link drops the surah when Quran tab is lazily mounted (clearPending batched with the navigation dispatch)
confirmed.description: AppStack's pendingSurah effect dispatches the tab navigation AND calls clearPending() in the same effect body. With React 19 automatic batching, both state updates (nav state + pendingSurah=null) land in a single commit. @react-navigation/bottom-tabs v7 mounts tab screens lazily (lazy defaults to true), so if the user has never visited the Quran tab this session, QuranTab mounts during that very commit — by which point pendingSurah is already null. QuranTab's useState initializer (`pendingSurah !== null ? pendingSurah : undefined`) therefore captures null, and QuranScreen mounts with initialSurah=undefined: the user lands on the surah list instead of the requested surah. The trigger is PrayerRow's 'Read Surah →' button (src/components/PrayerRow.tsx:103), which calls only `openSurah(surah.number)` with no navigate() — it relies entirely on this racy AppStack effect. By contrast TodayCard.tsx:74-75 calls openSurah AND navigate in the same handler, which mounts QuranTab while pendingSurah is still set, so that path works — explaining why this fails only 'sometimes' (only on the first Quran-tab entry of a session, only via PrayerRow). Once the Quran tab has been mounted (keep-alive), QuranTab's own effect runs before the parent AppStack effect and the feature works. This intermittent first-use failure matches the user complaint 'sometimes random things fail'.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Don't clear pendingSurah from AppStack. Let QuranTab own the lifecycle: keep pendingSurah set until QuranTab has actually consumed it (call clearPending from inside QuranTab's effect after setActiveSurah), or make PrayerRow navigate explicitly like TodayCard does. Simplest robust fix: in AppStack only dispatch navigation; in QuranTab's useEffect, when pendingSurah !== null, setActiveSurah + bump mountKey + clearPending().
confirmed.correction: Claim is accurate as written. Only nuance: severity could be argued medium-high rather than high, since the failure mode is landing on the Quran surah list (one extra tap to recover) rather than a crash or data loss. The fix is straightforward: have PrayerRow also navigate in the press handler (like TodayCard.tsx:74-75), or defer clearPending so it doesn't batch with the lazy tab mount (e.g. clear from QuranTab after consuming the value).

confirmed.title: QuranTab double-mounts QuranScreen (double surah fetch) on first deep-link entry
confirmed.description: When QuranTab mounts with pendingSurah already set (TodayCard path: openSurah + navigate batched in the same tap handler), the useState initializer already captures the surah into activeSurah with mountKey=0. The mount effect then runs, sees pendingSurah !== null, and increments mountKey 0→1, which changes the `key` on QuranScreen and unmounts/remounts it. The first QuranScreen instance's mount effect (QuranScreen.tsx:556-562) has already kicked off `openSurah(surah)` → `fetchSurah()`, so the surah is fetched twice and the screen visibly resets once on every deep-link into a freshly mounted Quran tab.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Skip the mountKey bump on the initial run, e.g. track a ref of the last-consumed pendingSurah, or only increment mountKey when pendingSurah differs from activeSurah / when the component is past its first render (useRef(false) firstRun guard).
confirmed.correction: The double-mount of QuranScreen on first deep-link entry into a freshly mounted Quran tab is real (App.tsx:150-162), but "the surah is fetched twice" is wrong at the network level: fetchSurah dedupes concurrent calls via an inflight map and a disk cache (src/services/quran.ts:85-104), so the second openSurah joins the same request or reads cache. "Screen visibly resets" is also overstated — the remount occurs in the same effect-flush cycle before any content loads, so the user-visible impact is at most a one-frame flicker. Actual cost: one wasted mount/unmount cycle and duplicate openSurah bookkeeping (saveLastRead, deduped prefetches).

confirmed.title: Onboarding defaults pair Karachi calculation method with Shafi madhab — wrong Asr time (~1h early) for the Hanafi-majority default audience
confirmed.description: The onboarding defaults are calculationMethod='Karachi' (labelled 'Pakistan, Bangladesh, India' in prayerMethods.ts:30) and madhab='Shafi'. Pakistan/India/Bangladesh follow the Hanafi madhab almost universally, where Asr is one mithl later (~45-75 min) than the Shafi calculation. Both 'Skip — choose later' buttons call finish(), which persists these defaults via storage.setPrayerSettings, so every user who skips (or taps through accepting defaults) gets an Asr time roughly an hour earlier than their local mosque — for the exact regional audience the Karachi default targets. The madhab list is also below the fold on step 2, so many users never see it.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Default selectedMadhab to 'Hanafi' when the method is 'Karachi' (or derive the madhab default from the chosen calculation method's region), keeping 'Shafi' for Middle-East/SE-Asia methods.
confirmed.correction: Claim is accurate as stated. Two minor additions: (1) the issue is slightly worse than described — MADHABS in prayerMethods.ts labels the Shafi option's region as 'Most of Middle East, South Asia, Southeast Asia', actively steering South Asian users toward the wrong Asr option even if they do scroll to it; (2) the persistence in finish() is not strictly the root cause, since all runtime fallbacks (usePrayerTimes, notifications, PrayerTrackerScreen) independently default to Shafi — fixing only the onboarding default would leave the same behavior for users where persistence fails.

confirmed.title: 3s onboarding-flag timeout re-onboards slow-storage users, and finishing onboarding then overwrites their saved prayer settings with defaults
confirmed.description: If AsyncStorage.getItem('onboarding_complete') takes longer than 3 seconds (the code's own comment cites corrupted SQLite / locked DB / extreme load — i.e. transient slowness, not just corruption), the timeout marks the launch as settled with onboardingDone=false and the `settled` guard discards the late-arriving true value. The returning user is forced through onboarding again, and OnboardingScreen.finish() unconditionally writes setFiqhSchool + setPrayerSettings with the in-screen defaults (sunni/Karachi/Shafi) — including via the 'Skip — choose later' button. A user who had carefully chosen e.g. ISNA + Hanafi silently has their calculation method and madhab replaced, shifting all their prayer times and notification schedule. The re-show was a deliberate tradeoff per the comment, but the settings overwrite on the resulting skip/finish path is silent data loss layered on top of it.
confirmed.suggestedFix: In finish(), read existing prayer settings first and only write when none exist (or pre-seed the onboarding selections from stored settings so a re-onboarded user's choices are preserved). Optionally raise the timeout or retry the getItem once before falling back.
confirmed.correction: Claim is accurate as stated. Minor scope note: the settings overwrite is not specific to the 3s timeout — any path that re-shows onboarding (getItem rejection at App.tsx 384-388, or a failed onboarding_complete write at App.tsx 490-496) plus a Skip/finish tap clobbers saved prayer settings the same way, because OnboardingScreen never seeds its state from stored settings. The root fix is hydrating OnboardingScreen defaults from storage (or skipping the writes on the Skip path), not just the timeout.

confirmed.title: Global context storage reads/writes have no rejection handling (unhandled promise rejections)
confirmed.description: SimpleModeContext's hydrate read has no .catch, so a failed AsyncStorage read becomes an unhandled promise rejection at app root. Same pattern in LanguageContext.tsx:26 (getItem without catch). Additionally, LanguageContext.setLanguage awaits AsyncStorage.setItem and is invoked fire-and-forget from the Sidebar language picker (Sidebar.tsx:383 `setLanguage(l.code)`) and OnboardingScreen language cards (OnboardingScreen.tsx:97 `onPress={() => setLanguage('en')}`), so a setItem failure there is also an unhandled rejection — and the language silently won't persist across restarts with no user feedback. Every other storage call in App.tsx is carefully .catch()-wrapped; these three are the stragglers.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Append .catch(() => {}) to the hydrate reads, and wrap the setItem in setLanguage/toggleSimpleMode in try/catch so the in-memory state still updates when persistence fails.
confirmed.correction: Accurate overall, with one mitigation missed: in OnboardingScreen, finish() (lines 62-80) re-runs `await setLanguage(language)` inside try/catch before completing onboarding, so language persistence in the onboarding flow gets a guarded retry — the 'silently won't persist' consequence applies fully only to the Sidebar picker. The unhandled rejections from the fire-and-forget onPress calls are still real in both places.

confirmed.title: iOS AdMob app ID in app.json is Google's published sample/test app ID
confirmed.description: The react-native-google-mobile-ads plugin config sets iosAppId to ca-app-pub-3940256099942544~1458002511, which is Google's documented sample AdMob app ID for iOS demos (the 3940256099942544 publisher ID is Google's test account). The androidAppId is a real one. Currently harmless because the app ships Android-only on Google Play, but any future iOS build would silently ship with test ad configuration: no revenue, and an AdMob policy violation if real traffic hits it. The infoPlist also declares UIBackgroundModes:["audio"] for that same iOS target.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Replace iosAppId with a real AdMob iOS app ID before any iOS release, or add a comment/tracking issue so it isn't shipped by accident.

confirmed.title: expo-notifications plugin has no Android notification icon/color configured
confirmed.description: The expo-notifications config plugin only sets "sounds": [] — no "icon" (monochrome small icon) or "color". Without a dedicated notification icon resource, Android falls back to the app icon, which the system renders as a flat silhouette in the status bar/shade; for a full-color launcher icon this typically appears as a solid grey/white square. For an app whose core retention loop is prayer-time, streak, and Jumu'ah notifications, every notification shows with a degraded generic glyph instead of a recognizable Noor mark.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add a white-on-transparent monochrome icon and brand color to the plugin config, e.g. { "icon": "./assets/notification-icon.png", "color": "#C8780A", "sounds": [] }, then rebuild.

confirmed.title: Prayer reminder notifications are hardcoded English for all languages
confirmed.description: The most frequent notification in the app (5x daily prayer reminders, scheduled by both schedulePrayerNotificationsForDays and schedulePrayerNotifications) uses a hardcoded English title and body. Urdu and Arabic users — who selected their language at onboarding and whose in-app prayer names ARE localized (translations.ts has fajr/dhuhr/asr/maghrib/isha in ur and ar) — receive English notifications every day. STREAK_COPY and JUMUAH_COPY in the same file are properly localized in all three languages, so this is an inconsistency, not a deliberate policy.
confirmed.evidence:
title: `⏰ ${prayer.name} in 5 minutes`,
          body: 'Time to prepare for prayer.',  (notifications.ts:131-132, repeated verbatim at 164-165)
confirmed.suggestedFix: Thread the Language param into schedulePrayerNotifications/schedulePrayerNotificationsForDays (same pattern as scheduleStreakReminder) and add a PRAYER_COPY: Record<Language, ...> map using the already-localized prayer names from translations.ts.
confirmed.correction: Substantially accurate, two minor details: (1) the function is schedulePrayerNotificationsAhead, not schedulePrayerNotificationsForDays; (2) the second function, schedulePrayerNotifications (lines 147-179, identical hardcoded copy), has no callers in the repo — only schedulePrayerNotificationsAhead is on the live path (via rebuildPrayerScheduleFromStorage from App.tsx and directly from PrayerTrackerScreen.tsx:177). The user-facing behavior described is correct.

confirmed.title: Streak/Jumuah notifications keep firing in the old language after a language switch
confirmed.description: scheduleStreakReminder and scheduleJumuahReminder are called exactly once, in a startup effect gated on [fontsReady, onboardingDone], with the language read directly from AsyncStorage at that moment. When the user later changes language from the Sidebar language picker (Sidebar.tsx:383 setLanguage(l.code)), nothing reschedules these repeating notifications — grep confirms App.tsx:442-443 are the only call sites. The daily streak reminder and weekly Jumuah reminder continue firing in the previous language until the next cold start of the app. Also note the storage key 'app_language' is duplicated here as a raw literal instead of being shared with LanguageContext's STORAGE_KEY.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Call scheduleStreakReminder(lang)/scheduleJumuahReminder(lang) inside LanguageContext.setLanguage (or an effect keyed on language) so repeating notifications are rebuilt in the newly selected language.

confirmed.title: Arabic-language users get fully English screens: Umrah, Hajj, Janaza, Dua, and Eid guides only branch on Urdu
confirmed.description: Arabic is offered as a first-class language at onboarding (OnboardingScreen.tsx:120-128) and newer screens (QurbaniScreen, TakbirScreen, SacredJourneyScreen, EidHubCard, TodayCard) handle isArabic. But five entire content screens derive only `isUrdu` and fall back to English for everything — titles, step content, duas, buttons: UmrahGuideScreen.tsx:24, HajjGuideScreen.tsx:24, JanazaScreen.tsx:26, DuaScreen.tsx:23, EidScreen.tsx:146. An Arabic user sees 'Umrah Guide', 'Mark as Complete', 'Masail, Rulings & Duas' etc. in English while the rest of the app (tab bar, sidebar, prayer tracker) is Arabic — a jarring mixed-language experience on exactly the screens an Arabic speaker is most likely to use.
confirmed.suggestedFix: At minimum localize the screen chrome (titles, buttons, labels) to Arabic via the existing translations.ts pattern; longer term add *Ar fields to the guide content constants like qurbani.ts already does (nameAr, sharesNoteAr, etc.).
confirmed.correction: The claim is accurate but understates the fix scope: this is not only a screen-code branching gap. The underlying content data (e.g., src/constants/umrahGuide.ts) defines only English + Urdu fields (title/titleUr, description/descriptionUr, tips/tipsUr) with no *Ar variants — `titleAr` exists only in dailyAct.ts, TodayCard.tsx, and Sidebar.tsx. Adding an isArabic branch alone cannot fix the content; Arabic translations would need to be authored for the guide data, or at minimum the UI chrome (titles, buttons like 'Mark as Complete') localized.

confirmed.title: HifzScreen and AsmaulHusnaScreen are completely unlocalized (no language context at all)
confirmed.description: These are the only two screens in src/screens that never import useLanguage (verified with grep -L). All user-facing strings are hardcoded English: HifzScreen header/status labels, and AsmaulHusnaScreen's detail panel, dhikr card, and the 'Recitation is on the way' modal. Urdu/Arabic users navigating from the (localized) Sidebar land on fully English screens.
confirmed.evidence:
<Text style={styles.headerTitle}>Hifz Tracker</Text>
<Text style={styles.headerSub}>Track your Quran memorization</Text>  (HifzScreen.tsx:119-120; also 'Not started' at line 36)
{known.has(selected.number) ? '✓ I know this' : 'Mark as known'} ... <Text style={styles.dhikrTitle}>Daily Dhikr</Text>
<Text style={styles.dhikrSub}>Recite this name 100 times after Fajr prayer</Text>  (AsmaulHusnaScreen.tsx:184, 196-197, plus 'Recitation is on the way'/'Stay tuned'/'Got it' at 228/235/243)
confirmed.suggestedFix: Import useLanguage and add the handful of strings to translations.ts (or use the isUrdu/isArabic ternary pattern used by sibling screens).

confirmed.title: Today's Goals are persisted in the first-run language and never migrate after a language switch
confirmed.description: On first Home focus, the default goals ('Read Quran 10 minutes', 'Dhikr after Fajr', ...) are written to storage in whatever language is active at that moment, and on every subsequent focus the stored copies win unconditionally. DEFAULT_GOALS_BY_LANG has en/ur/ar variants (lines 39-52), but they are only ever consulted when storage is empty. A user who starts in English and later switches to Urdu keeps English goal texts on an otherwise-Urdu home screen forever (until goal text is manually edited or storage is reset). The focus effect even re-runs on [language] (line 156), but the stored-goals branch ignores the new language.
confirmed.suggestedFix: On language change, detect goals whose id+text exactly match a DEFAULT_GOALS_BY_LANG entry of any language and remap them to the new language's default text (user-customized goals stay untouched).
confirmed.correction: Claim is accurate except one detail that understates it: there is no goal-editing UI (GoalItem only toggles completion; no TextInput/prompt exists in HomeScreen), so the stale-language goal texts persist until app data is cleared — not "until goal text is manually edited." Additionally, LanguageContext defaults to 'en' and hydrates the stored language asynchronously, so a non-English user's first Home focus can race and seed English goals even without ever switching languages. Minor nits: default text is "Read 10 min of Quran" (not "Read Quran 10 minutes") and DEFAULT_GOALS_BY_LANG spans lines 37-53, not 39-52.

confirmed.title: Mood coach responses are English-only regardless of app language
confirmed.description: MoodScreen's UI is fully localized via t (moodCoach, moodSubtitle, coachThinking all have ur/ar entries in translations.ts), but the actual coach response — the main content of the screen — comes from getLocalMotivation, which takes no language parameter and draws from English-only RESPONSES/MISSED_NOTES pools plus English streak suffixes. An Urdu user taps a mood under an Urdu header and receives a long English paragraph. The English response is also persisted into mood history (MoodScreen.tsx:67 aiResponse: response).
confirmed.suggestedFix: Pass language into getLocalMotivation and add ur/ar response pools (the copy volume is small — 5 mood levels x a few variants).

confirmed.title: Language load has no error handling and can overwrite an early user selection
confirmed.description: Two issues in the language hydration path. (1) AsyncStorage.getItem has no .catch — this app has documented AsyncStorage/SQLite failures (App.tsx:415-419 describes SQLITE_FULL blocking writes), and a rejected read produces an unhandled promise rejection while silently leaving an Urdu/Arabic user's app in English. (2) Race: if the user calls setLanguage before the initial getItem resolves (e.g. tapping a language card on the onboarding screen, which is the very first screen and calls setLanguage directly at OnboardingScreen.tsx:97/109/121), the late-resolving getItem callback unconditionally calls setLanguageState(stored), reverting the user's fresh choice to the previously stored value. There is no flag to skip the hydration result once setLanguage has run.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add .catch(() => {}) and a `selectedByUserRef` (set in setLanguage) that the hydration callback checks before applying the stored value.
confirmed.correction: Real but low severity, not medium. Accurate version: LanguageContext hydration lacks a .catch (a failed read logs an unhandled rejection and silently leaves the default 'en' — cosmetic fallback, no crash) and lacks a guard to discard the hydration result if setLanguage ran first. The onboarding-revert race is theoretical only: on first launch stored is null so the guard prevents any overwrite, and in the rare re-onboarding edge case the window is milliseconds after provider mount (which itself is behind the splash gate). Worth a one-line .catch and a hydration-skip flag as hardening, but no plausible user-facing revert on the cited onboarding path.

confirmed.title: I18nProvider is dead code — its RTL helpers have zero consumers, leaving RTL handling ad hoc and inconsistent
confirmed.description: This answers the two-contexts question: LanguageContext does all real work (persistence + t strings); I18nProvider merely derives RTL helpers from it. But grep across src/ and App.tsx shows useI18n is imported nowhere — the provider wraps the tree (App.tsx:512) and nothing reads it. Its writingDirection/flexDirection/textAlign values are never applied, and the 'Keep I18nManager in sync' comment is a literal no-op (`void I18nManager;`). Consequence: RTL is reimplemented per-component, inconsistently — e.g. HadithOfDay picks Arabic hadith text for ar (line 19-21) but right-aligns only for Urdu, so Arabic hadith body text renders left-aligned: textAlign: language === 'ur' ? 'right' : 'left' (HadithOfDay.tsx:33). They don't conflict (I18n is derived from Language), but one of them is pure dead weight.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Either adopt useI18n in the components currently hand-rolling isUrdu/isArabic ternaries (fixing gaps like HadithOfDay's Arabic alignment), or delete the provider.
confirmed.correction: Mostly accurate as stated. One nuance: the claim implies the 'Keep I18nManager in sync' comment promises behavior it fails to deliver; in fact the full comment explicitly documents the no-op as intentional ('we render bidirectionally instead of restarting the bundle... This is intentional: we don't want a relaunch loop'). So the I18nManager no-op is deliberate and documented, not an accidental gap — but the core finding (I18nProvider has zero consumers and RTL is handled ad hoc, e.g. Arabic hadith body left-aligned at HadithOfDay.tsx:33) is correct.

confirmed.title: QiblaScreen 'Facing Qibla' banner skips Arabic despite the screen otherwise being fully localized
confirmed.description: QiblaScreen uses t for its strings (qiblaDirection, turnLeft, turnRight etc. all have ar entries in translations.ts), but the success banner — the emotional payoff moment of the screen — is a hardcoded ur/en ternary, so Arabic users see English exactly when alignment is achieved.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add facingQibla/beginPrayer keys to translations.ts with ar entries and use t, matching the rest of the screen.

confirmed.title: Sacred-event milestone notifications mix an Arabic title with an English verse body
confirmed.description: buildMilestoneCopy localizes the title for ar (lines 374-390 have explicit Arabic branches) and picks nameAr, but the verse appended to every body falls back to English for Arabic users because SacredQuote (islamicCalendar.ts:65-69) only has en/ur fields. Result: notification title in Arabic, body verse in English.
confirmed.evidence: const verse = language === 'ur' ? quote.ur : quote.en;  (notifications.ts:368; SacredQuote type at islamicCalendar.ts:65-69 is { arabic?: string; en: string; ur: string; source: string } — no ar field)
confirmed.suggestedFix: For language === 'ar', prefer quote.arabic (the original Arabic verse text) when present, falling back to en.
confirmed.correction: Two refinements: (1) the SacredQuote type lives in src/constants/islamicCalendar.ts, not src/services/islamicCalendar.ts; (2) the issue is slightly broader than claimed — for Arabic users the hook line ALSO falls back to English (hookFor does `hookLocalized(...) ?? en` and hookLocalized only handles 'ur'), so the entire notification body is English under an Arabic title. A code comment at notifications.ts:501 ('Arabic falls through to the Qur'an quote alone — intentionally short') contradicts the actual behavior, since the English hook is still appended and the English verse (not the available `arabic` original) is shown.

confirmed.title: Sidebar share-failure alert has no Arabic branch
confirmed.description: Sidebar consistently handles isUrdu/isArabic everywhere (e.g. onResetStatsOnly at lines 186-193 has all three languages), but the catch branch of onShareApp only checks isUrdu, so Arabic users get an English error alert. Minor, but it is the only ur-only ternary in an otherwise tri-lingual file.
confirmed.evidence: Alert.alert(isUrdu ? 'خرابی' : 'Error', isUrdu ? 'شیئر نہیں ہو سکا' : 'Could not share app');  (Sidebar.tsx:178)
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add the isArabic branch: isUrdu ? 'خرابی' : isArabic ? 'خطأ' : 'Error', etc.
confirmed.correction: The core finding is correct, but the description's claim that this is "the only ur-only ternary in an otherwise tri-lingual file" is false. The same ur-only pattern also appears at lines 293 and 296 (store-link failure alerts: isUrdu ? 'خرابی' : 'Error' / 'لنک نہیں کھل سکا' : 'Could not open store link') and at line 357 (Close button label: isUrdu ? 'بند کریں' : 'Close'). The Arabic-missing-branch issue exists in four spots in Sidebar.tsx, not one.

confirmed.title: Home screen daily quote is English-only for Urdu/Arabic users
confirmed.description: The rotating Quran quote card on the (otherwise localized) Home screen draws from MOTIVATIONAL_QUOTES whose QuoteEntry type is { text: string; source: string } with English-only text — no ur/ar variants exist and no language is consulted when rendering. Urdu/Arabic users see an English quote front-and-center on the home screen every day.
confirmed.evidence:
export type QuoteEntry = { text: string; source: string };  (quotes.ts:127; entries like { text: 'Verily, with hardship comes ease.', source: 'Quran 94:6' } at quotes.ts:2)
{quote.text} ... — {quote.source}  (HomeScreen.tsx:275, 282 — rendered with no language branch)
confirmed.suggestedFix: Extend QuoteEntry with ur/ar fields (these are Quran verses — established Urdu translations exist) and select by language in HomeScreen.

confirmed.title: Note: no missing-key risk and language switch propagates correctly to keep-alive screens
confirmed.description: Two audit questions verified as NON-issues, recorded for the parent agent: (1) Missing keys at runtime are impossible — t is a strictly typed Translations object (translations.ts:9-87) and all three language records must satisfy it; `npx tsc --noEmit` passes clean, and a key-by-key grep found no t.* reference without a definition. The only staleness is the reverse direction: 9 defined keys are never used (you, qiblaLabel, learnTab, umrahTab, hajjTab, janazaTab, qiblaTab, moodTab, statsTab — leftovers from a previous tab layout; App.tsx:197-199 only uses homeTab/prayersTab/quranTab). (2) Keep-alive screens DO re-render on language switch: every screen reads t/language via useLanguage() context, and a context value change re-renders all mounted consumers regardless of focus — the stale-on-return bug class does not apply to language strings. The genuine language-switch gaps are the persisted/scheduled artifacts reported separately (stored goal texts, scheduled notifications).
confirmed.evidence:
export type Translations = { greeting: string; ... }  (translations.ts:9-87, strict record; tsc --noEmit exits 0)
UNUSED keys per grep: you, qiblaLabel, learnTab, umrahTab, hajjTab, janazaTab, qiblaTab, moodTab, statsTab
confirmed.suggestedFix: Optionally delete the 9 unused keys (x3 languages) to keep the file honest; no runtime action needed.

confirmed.title: useCompass bypasses the permission-request dedup, recreating the documented Android concurrent-request bug on the Qibla screen
confirmed.description: QiblaScreen mounts useLocation() and useCompass() simultaneously. useLocation requests permission through the module-level dedup wrapper requestLocationPermissionOnce(), whose own comment documents that on Android a second concurrent requestForegroundPermissionsAsync() call returns 'denied' even after the user just granted. useCompass ignores that wrapper and calls Location.requestForegroundPermissionsAsync() directly, so on the first-ever open of the Qibla screen (permission undetermined) the two requests race. Depending on which loses, either useCompass sets available=false (showing the 'Compass needs location permission' card even though the user granted) or useLocation falls back to Karachi coordinates. This is exactly the bug class the wrapper was added to fix, and it matches the 'random things fail' complaint.
confirmed.suggestedFix: In useCompass, replace the direct Location.requestForegroundPermissionsAsync() call with requestLocationPermissionOnce() imported from useLocation.ts (or move the wrapper to a shared module).
confirmed.correction: The bypass of the dedup wrapper is real and the race is real, but it does NOT occur on first-ever Qibla open with undetermined permission — App.tsx:449 resolves the permission at first launch before Qibla is reachable, and when permission is already granted useCompass's getForegroundPermissionsAsync check (line 22-23) prevents any second request. The actual failure path: user denies or dismisses the launch prompt, later opens Qibla; useLocation (via requestLocationPermissionOnce) and useCompass (direct call) then issue concurrent requestForegroundPermissionsAsync calls; if the user grants this Android re-prompt, the losing call returns 'denied', so either the compass shows the 'needs location permission' card despite the grant or useLocation falls back to Karachi. Severity is medium, not high — it only affects Android users in the deny-then-grant-on-Qibla path. Fix: have useCompass use requestLocationPermissionOnce() from useLocation.ts.

confirmed.title: Qibla screen silently shows Karachi's qibla bearing as if it were the user's when location is denied or unavailable
confirmed.description: useLocation exposes usingFallback, error, and permissionDenied, but QiblaScreen destructures only { location, loading } and additionally hard-codes the Karachi fallback again with `?? 24.8607`. When permission is denied (or getCurrentPositionAsync throws, e.g. location services off) with no cached coords, useLocation returns KARACHI_FALLBACK and the screen confidently renders Karachi's qibla bearing (~267° W) in the big angle text and draws the Kaaba arrow at that bearing — for a user in London the true bearing is ~119°, in New York ~58°. In the GPS-error case (permission granted, fix failed) the compass sensor still works, so there is no warning of any kind: the screen looks fully functional while pointing the user the wrong way for prayer.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Destructure usingFallback/permissionDenied/error/retry in QiblaScreen; when usingFallback is true, replace the confident angle display with a prominent 'location unavailable — direction may be wrong' warning plus a retry button, instead of rendering the fallback city's bearing.
confirmed.correction: One nuance: the permission-DENIED case is not fully silent. useCompass independently requests the same foreground permission, so denial sets available=false, which renders the "Compass needs location permission" card (QiblaScreen.tsx:226-243). However, the Karachi bearing is still displayed prominently above that card, and the card's copy ("only used to compute the Qibla bearing") implies the shown bearing is location-based when it is actually the Karachi default — so the denied path warns about the compass but still misrepresents the bearing. The fully-silent failure is the granted-permission/failed-fix/no-cache path, which the claim describes accurately. Severity high is reasonable for a prayer-direction feature.

confirmed.title: Permission card sends user to Settings but the screen never re-checks permission on return — fix appears not to work
confirmed.description: When permission is denied, useCompass sets available=false and QiblaScreen shows a card that says 'Tap to open Settings and enable location'. The user taps, grants permission in Settings, and returns to the app — but useCompass's effect runs once on mount ([] deps) with no AppState 'active' listener and no focus-driven re-check, and useLocation likewise only re-runs via retry() which QiblaScreen never calls. So after following the app's own instructions the card is still shown, the heading never starts, and the qibla stays on the Karachi fallback until the user backs out of the screen and reopens it (the screen is a pushed stack screen, so returning from Settings does not remount it).
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add an AppState listener in useCompass (and trigger useLocation's retry) that re-runs getForegroundPermissionsAsync when the app returns to 'active', restarting the heading watcher if permission is now granted.
confirmed.correction: Accurate in substance, with two caveats: (1) on iOS, changing a privacy permission in Settings terminates the app, so relaunch remounts the hook and the compass recovers — the stuck-card loop primarily affects Android, where granting from Settings does not kill the process; (2) the user can recover by navigating back and re-entering the Qibla screen (remount), though the UI never says so. Severity is closer to medium-high than high: a real UX dead-end on Android, but recoverable and platform-limited.

confirmed.title: Heading watcher subscription leaks if the screen unmounts while watchHeadingAsync is still resolving
confirmed.description: The cleanup function calls watcher?.remove(), but watcher is only assigned after `await Location.watchHeadingAsync(...)` resolves. If the user opens Qibla and backs out before that promise settles (it also sits behind two awaited permission calls, so the window is real), cleanup runs while watcher is still null; the promise then resolves and assigns the subscription, which nothing ever removes. The callback's `if (!active) return` prevents setState but does not stop the native magnetometer/heading sensor, which keeps running for the rest of the app session — battery drain.
confirmed.suggestedFix: After the await: `watcher = await Location.watchHeadingAsync(cb); if (!active) { watcher.remove(); watcher = null; }`.
confirmed.correction: The bug is real, but the claimed window is overstated: the two awaited permission calls do NOT widen the leak window, because each is followed by an `if (!active) return` guard (lines 25 and 31) that prevents the watch from ever starting if the screen unmounted during them. The only leak window is the resolution time of the watchHeadingAsync call itself (a short native-bridge round trip), not the entire permission flow. Consequence (heading sensor running for the rest of the app session) is as described; trigger likelihood is lower than implied, so this sits at low-to-medium rather than solid medium severity. Fix is a one-liner: after the await, `if (!active) { watcher.remove(); watcher = null; }`.

confirmed.title: Silent fallback to magnetic heading when uncalibrated, with no calibration/interference warning despite the code's own accuracy caveat
confirmed.description: When trueHeading is -1 (sensor uncalibrated / magnetic interference), the hook silently substitutes magHeading and the UI renders it identically — including the 'Facing Qibla!' confirmation. The file's own header comment states a magnetic reading 'can be off from true north by up to ~20° in high-declination regions... which would invalidate the Qibla direction we draw on screen', yet the hook never surfaces which reading is in use, and the heading object's accuracy field is ignored entirely, so no 'calibrate your compass / move away from metal' prompt is ever shown. The audit-relevant failure: a user near a magnet or with an uncalibrated sensor gets a confident green 'Facing Qibla!' on a wrong heading.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Expose a lowAccuracy flag from useCompass (trueHeading < 0 or h.accuracy <= 1) and render a calibration banner ('wave phone in a figure-8 / move away from metal') in QiblaScreen, suppressing the 'Facing Qibla!' confirmation while accuracy is low.
confirmed.correction: Minor nuance only: the silent magHeading fallback itself is standard native-compass behavior (as the code comment notes) and is not the defect; the real gap is that the heading callback's accuracy field is ignored and no calibration/interference state is ever surfaced to the UI, so the 'Facing Qibla!' confirmation renders with full confidence on a potentially badly wrong magnetic reading.

confirmed.title: Qibla screen blocks on full-screen loading even when a cached location exists, and the GPS fix has no timeout
confirmed.description: useLocation sets the cached location immediately but keeps loading=true until getCurrentPositionAsync resolves (loading is only cleared in the finally block). QiblaScreen early-returns a bare 'Getting location…' text whenever loading is true, so a returning user with a perfectly good cached location stares at a blank loading screen for as long as the GPS fix takes — and getCurrentPositionAsync is called with no timeout or maximumAge-style guard, so on a slow/indoor fix this can be many seconds (contributing to 'sometimes random things fail' reports). The compass could have been rendered instantly from cache.
confirmed.suggestedFix: In useLocation, set loading=false as soon as cached coords are applied (treat the GPS fix as a background refresh), and/or race getCurrentPositionAsync against a ~8s timeout falling back to Location.getLastKnownPositionAsync().
confirmed.correction: Core claim is accurate. Two minor nuances: (1) since tab screens in this app stay mounted, the block occurs once per mount/app-session, not on every tab revisit; (2) the link to 'sometimes random things fail' user reports is speculative attribution — the confirmed defect is a UX/perceived-reliability stall, not a functional failure. Medium severity remains fair.

confirmed.title: Unthrottled, unsmoothed heading updates re-render the entire SVG compass at raw sensor rate
confirmed.description: Every heading event calls setHeading with the raw float value — no low-pass filter, no rounding, no minimum-delta threshold. watchHeadingAsync fires on sub-degree changes at high frequency, and each state change re-renders QiblaScreen's full Svg tree (72 tick <Line>s, 8 cardinal labels, 8 degree labels, the qibla arrow group) because ringRotation = -heading changes every event. On low-end Android devices this produces a visibly jittery needle and sustained JS-thread load while the screen is open; there is no smoothing anywhere between the sensor and the transform string.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Skip updates where |new - old| < ~0.8° (wrap-aware), or apply an exponential low-pass filter; alternatively drive the rotation through a Reanimated shared value so sensor updates bypass React re-renders.
confirmed.correction: Accurate version: every heading event (capped natively on Android at ~20 Hz and >=~2 degree deltas by expo-location's DEGREE_DELTA/TIME_DELTA filter; uncapped on iOS where no headingFilter is set) calls setHeading with an unrounded float and triggers a full re-render of QiblaScreen's SVG tree (72 tick Lines, 16 labels, arrow group) with no smoothing or animation. On Android the visible artifact is unanimated ~2-degree step-snapping during rotation rather than sub-degree high-frequency jitter; sustained JS load while rotating is real but bounded at 20 Hz. Severity is closer to low-medium than medium; the 'visibly jittery needle ... sub-degree changes at high frequency' description applies only to iOS.

confirmed.title: Eid Guide skips to next year's Eid on the day of Eid itself
confirmed.description: getNextEid() uses a strict `e.date > now` comparison against a midnight-anchored Date. From 00:00 on Eid day onward, the current Eid no longer matches, so on the most-trafficked day of the year the countdown card shows next year's Eid (e.g. 'Eid ul-Fitr 2027 — 287 DAYS' on the morning of Eid al-Adha) and `activeTab` defaults to the WRONG Eid type ('fitr' content on Eid al-Adha day). This contradicts the Home screen's EidHubCard, which correctly shows 'Today' on the day of Eid and lingers 5 days after. Users opening the Eid Guide on Eid morning for salah/takbeer instructions land on the wrong tab with a misleading countdown.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Treat an Eid as 'current' through its day (and ideally a few tashreeq days for Adha): find((e) => endOfDay(e.date, graceDays) > now). On day-of, show 'Today / Eid Mubarak' instead of a countdown, and keep activeTab on the current Eid's type.
confirmed.correction: Claim is accurate as stated. Minor nuances: the countdown card is literally labeled 'NEXT EID', so the text is self-consistent even if misleading; Adha content remains one tap away via the tab toggle (wrong default, not inaccessible content). Severity 'high' is defensible given it triggers precisely on the highest-traffic day, though 'medium-high' is arguable.

confirmed.title: Takbir and Qurbani windows ignore the user's Hijri offset setting
confirmed.description: The app has a whole Hijri-offset system (PrayerSettingsModal stepper, storage.getHijriOffset, computeHijriOffsetFromServer auto-correction used by HomeScreen) precisely because the tabular algorithm drifts ±1-2 days from regional moonsighting. But TakbirScreen and QurbaniScreen call gregorianToHijri(new Date()) with NO offset. So a Pakistani user whose Home screen Hijri date is corrected (+1) gets a Takbir tracker whose 'Active now — Day X Dhul Hijjah' banner, TODAY row highlight, and 23-prayer checklist day-keys are off by a day for the entire 9–13 Dhul Hijjah window — the exact 5 days the feature exists for. Same for the Qurbani screen's 'Qurbani window is open' / don't-cut-hair banners. These windows are religiously time-sensitive (Takbir after Asr on the 13th stops; slaughter ends at sunset on the 13th).
confirmed.suggestedFix: Load the stored offset on focus/mount (const off = await storage.getHijriOffset()) and pass it: gregorianToHijri(new Date(), off) in both TakbirScreen and QurbaniScreen, matching HomeScreen/TodayCard behavior.
